User.title: User
User.description: Represents a user account in the ChatServe Bot application, authenticated via phone number.

id.description: Unique identifier for the User entity.

phoneNumber.description: The user's unique phone number, used for authentication (e.g., +15551234567).

createdAt.description: Timestamp indicating when the user account was created.

Service.title: Service
Service.description: Defines an SMM service offered by the ChatServe Bot, including its platform, name, description, and pricing details.

id.description: Unique identifier for the Service entity.

platform.description: The social media platform the service targets (e.g., 'Instagram', 'YouTube').

name.description: The specific name of the service (e.g., 'Followers', 'Likes', 'Views').

description.description: A brief description explaining what the service offers.

unitPrice.description: The price for a single unit of the service (e.g., per follower, per like).

minimumQuantity.description: The minimum number of units a user can order for this service.

maximumQuantity.description: The maximum number of units a user can order for this service.

Order.title: Order
Order.description: Represents a user's placement of an SMM service order through the ChatServe Bot.

id.description: Unique identifier for the Order entity.

userId.description: Reference to the User who placed this order. (Relationship: User 1:N Order)

serviceId.description: Reference to the Service that was ordered. (Relationship: Service 1:N Order)

link.description: The URL of the social media content or profile where the service is to be delivered.

quantity.description: The requested quantity of the service (e.g., number of followers, likes).

totalPrice.description: The total calculated price for this order.

status.description: The current processing status of the order (e.g., 'Pending', 'Processing', 'Completed', 'Cancelled').

createdAt.description: Timestamp indicating when the order was initially placed.

updatedAt.description: Timestamp indicating when the order's status or details were last updated.

ChatMessage.title: ChatMessage
ChatMessage.description: Represents a single message sent either by the user or the bot within a specific chat conversation.

id.description: Unique identifier for the ChatMessage entity.

userId.description: Reference to the User associated with this chat conversation. (Relationship: User 1:N ChatMessage)

sender.description: The originator of the message: 'user' for a message sent by the user, 'bot' for a message sent by the bot.

text.description: The actual content of the chat message.

timestamp.description: Timestamp indicating when the message was sent.

definition.description: Stores user profile data, including their phone number and creation timestamp. This is the root for all user-specific data. Owned by the user specified in `userId`.

params.name: userId
params.description: The unique Firebase Authentication UID of the user.

definition.description: Stores definitions of all available SMM services (e.g., Instagram Followers, YouTube Likes). These documents are globally readable by all authenticated users. Admin-only write access.

params.name: serviceId
params.description: The unique identifier for a specific SMM service.

definition.description: Stores individual SMM orders placed by a specific user. This subcollection ensures user-specific ownership. Includes denormalized 'userId' field matching the path for authorization independence and simplified security rules. Users can only create new orders, not modify existing ones. Admins have full access.

params.name: userId
params.description: The unique Firebase Authentication UID of the user who placed the order.

params.name: orderId
params.description: The unique identifier for the specific order.

definition.description: Stores chat messages between a user and the bot. This subcollection ensures privacy and direct ownership by the user. Includes denormalized 'userId' field matching the path for authorization independence and simplified security rules.

params.name: userId
params.description: The unique Firebase Authentication UID of the user participating in the chat.

params.name: chatMessageId
params.description: The unique identifier for a specific chat message.

definition.description: A top-level collection used for Database Access Control (DBAC) to identify administrators. The mere existence of a document at this path signifies that the corresponding `userId` has administrative privileges. Document content can be minimal or empty.

params.name: userId
params.description: The unique Firebase Authentication UID of a user designated as an administrator.

firestore.reasoning:
The Firestore structure is designed to be secure, scalable, and easily debuggable, strictly adhering to the provided core design principles and mandates. 

**Authorization Independence (Strategy A) & QAPs:**

1.  **Users (`/users/{userId}`):** User profile data is stored under their unique UID, enforcing Path-Based Ownership. Access rules are simple: `request.auth.uid == userId` allows a user to manage their own profile. Admin access is granted via a separate `roles_admin` collection, avoiding hierarchical dependencies. List operations are generally restricted or admin-only, as direct queries by UID are common for individual user data.
2.  **Services (`/services/{serviceId}`):** All SMM services are stored in a top-level collection. This collection has a homogeneous security posture: it's globally readable by all authenticated users. The `platform` and `name` are fields within each `Service` document, allowing for straightforward `list` operations (QAPs) by querying `where('platform', '==', 'Instagram')` without complex rules or `get()` calls. This structure avoids nesting services under platforms as distinct collections, which would deviate from the `Service` entity's schema definition.
3.  **User Orders (`/users/{userId}/orders/{orderId}`):** Orders are placed as subcollections under the respective user's document. This directly implements Path-Based Ownership (Strategy C.2) and Authorization Independence (Strategy A). The `Order` document includes `userId` which is denormalized and matches the `{userId}` wildcard in the path. This allows for extremely simple, atomic security rules like `allow read, write: if request.auth.uid == userId;` for users (with write-only for creation) and `allow read, write: if isAdmin();` for admins. List operations (QAPs) are naturally supported: a user can `list` their own orders by querying `/users/{request.auth.uid}/orders`, as the rules ensure `request.auth.uid` matches the path.
4.  **Chat Messages (`/users/{userId}/chatMessages/{chatMessageId}`):** Similar to orders, chat messages are stored as subcollections under the user's document, ensuring private, user-specific conversations. The `ChatMessage` document includes `userId`, denormalized to match the path. This structure provides Authorization Independence (Strategy A) and allows for simple, atomic security rules (`request.auth.uid == userId`). QAPs are achieved as users can only `list` messages within their own `/users/{request.auth.uid}/chatMessages` path.
5.  **Admin Roles (`/roles_admin/{userId}`):** This top-level collection utilizes the "Existence over Content" pattern for global roles (Strategy C.4). An admin's UID is simply a document ID in this collection. Security rules can check `exists(/databases/$(database)/documents/roles_admin/$(request.auth.uid))` to grant admin privileges. This is authorization-independent and ensures QAPs for checking admin status without needing to traverse complex hierarchies.

**Structural Segregation (Strategy B):** Each collection and subcollection (`users`, `services`, `users/{userId}/orders`, `users/{userId}/chatMessages`, `roles_admin`) maintains a homogeneous security posture, making rules straightforward and minimizing complexity. For instance, `services` are uniformly public-read, while `users/{userId}/orders` are uniformly owned by the respective user (or accessible by admin).

**Data Clarity and Predictability (Strategy D):** All paths use descriptive wildcards (e.g., `{userId}`, `{serviceId}`, `{orderId}`, `{chatMessageId}`) and consistent naming conventions. The `status` field in `Order` explicitly models state as a Finite State Machine.

Overall, this structure prioritizes security and debuggability by leveraging path-based authorization, denormalization of ownership, and structural segregation, making security rules concise and easy to verify.
